preFix.title: Stripping and repainting pine kitchen chairs
preFix.subtitle: Strip old paint from pine chairs and apply a flat chalk-style finish
preFix.excerpt: How to strip and repaint pine kitchen chairs: softening old paint with a heat gun or stripper, scraping back, sanding, priming, and finishing with a flat water-based chalk-style paint for a durable result.

content.text: A set of pine kitchen chairs with cracked, peeling, or dirty paint responds well to a full strip and a

finish. Chalk-type paints cut the work at the preparation stage: they adhere to bare wood without a coat of primer and require no sanding between coats. The preparation work is the stripping, not the painting.

Chairs painted before 1980 may contain lead paint. Use a lead paint test kit before stripping. If lead is present, use chemical stripper (not a heat gun which aerosolises lead dust), work outdoors or in a well-ventilated space, wear a P100 respirator, and dispose of the stripped material as controlled waste.

Apply water-based paint stripper generously to all surfaces; leave for 30 to 45 minutes until the paint blisters and wrinkles. Scrape off the softened paint with the 50 mm scraper on flat surfaces. For turned spindles and moulded sections, use coarse string or a strip of canvas worked back and forth. A second application deals with any remaining paint. On thick old paint buildups, the heat gun on flat areas blisters the paint faster; keep the gun moving to avoid scorching the pine.

Sand all flat surfaces with 120-grit sandpaper on the sanding block, working with the grain. Use the card scraper on any flat sections that show grain-raising or uneven stripper residue; a scraper leaves a cleaner surface than sandpaper on bare pine. Finish with 180-grit by hand. Wipe the entire chair with a damp cloth and allow to dry fully before painting.

Apply chalk-type paint directly to bare wood with the 25 mm brush. The first coat will appear patchy on absorbent pine: this is normal. Brush out in the direction of the grain. Allow to dry for one hour, sand lightly with 180-grit by hand to knock back any raised grain, then apply the second coat. Two full coats are sufficient for solid coverage on pine.

Apply a clear furniture wax or a satin water-based lacquer over the dry chalk paint. Wax gives a softer finish but must be renewed every year or two on frequently used chairs; lacquer is harder and more durable. Apply the sealant in a single thin coat and buff when dry. The sealer is not optional on kitchen chairs; unsealed chalk paint marks and scuffs immediately.

attrs.fix: Sand lightly with 180-grit between every coat to remove raised fibres. Ensure the wood is fully dry before applying each coat.
attrs.cause: The wood was not sanded between coats, or the wood was damp when painted.
attrs.symptom: Paint raises the grain badly on the second coat
